verses.text: This then was the lot of the tribe of the children of Judah by their families; even to the border of Edom the wilderness of Zin southward was the uttermost part of the south coast. Their south border was from the shore of the salt sea, from the bay that looketh southward: it went out to the south side to Maaleh-acrabbim, passed along to Zin, ascended up on the south side unto Kadesh-barnea, passed along to Hezron, went up to Adar, fetched a compass to Karkaa: from thence it passed toward Azmon, went out unto the river of Egypt; and the goings out of that coast were at the sea: this shall be your south coast. The east border was the salt sea, even unto the end of Jordan. Their border in the north quarter was from the bay of the sea at the uttermost part of Jordan: the border went up to Beth-hogla, passed along by the north of Beth-arabah; went up to the stone of Bohan the son of Reuben: the border went up toward Debir from the valley of Achor, and so northward, looking toward Gilgal, that is before the going up to Adummim, which is on the south side of the river: the border passed toward the waters of En-shemesh, the goings out thereof were at En-rogel: the border went up by the valley of the son of Hinnom unto the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem: the border went up to the top of the mountain that lieth before the valley of Hinnom westward, which is at the end of the valley of the giants northward: the border was drawn from the top of the hill unto the fountain of the water of Nephtoah, went out to the cities of mount Ephron; the border was drawn to Baalah, which is Kirjath-jearim: the border compassed from Baalah westward unto mount Seir, passed along unto the side of mount Jearim, which is Chesalon, on the north side, went down to Beth-shemesh, passed on to Timnah: the border went out unto the side of Ekron northward: the border was drawn to Shicron, passed along to mount Baalah, went out unto Jabneel; the goings out of the border were at the sea. The west border was to the great sea, and the coast thereof. This is the coast of the children of Judah round about by their families. Guidance: Judah's territory is drawn first, and it dwarfs the rest. From the Salt Sea on the east to the Mediterranean on the west, from the Negev wilderness to the Hinnom valley by Jerusalem — Judah is the largest tribe with the largest portion, the tribe from which Messiah will come (Gen 49:10). Note the Jebusite mention by name: 'the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem.' The chapter's last verse will name what is wrong with that. Borders are precise; the prophet of the future capital is already inside the deed.

Guidance: Judah's territory is drawn first, and it dwarfs the rest. From the Salt Sea on the east to the Mediterranean on the west, from the Negev wilderness to the Hinnom valley by Jerusalem — Judah is the largest tribe with the largest portion, the tribe from which Messiah will come (Gen 49:10). Note the Jebusite mention by name: 'the south side of the Jebusite; the same is Jerusalem.' The chapter's last verse will name what is wrong with that. Borders are precise; the prophet of the future capital is already inside the deed.

Unto Caleb the son of Jephunneh he gave a part among the children of Judah, according to the commandment of the LORD to Joshua, even the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron. Caleb drove thence the three sons of Anak: Sheshai, Ahiman, Talmai, the children of Anak. From thence he went up to the inhabitants of Debir: the name of Debir before was Kirjath-sepher. Caleb said: He that smiteth Kirjath-sepher, and taketh it, to him will I give Achsah my daughter to wife. Othniel the son of Kenaz, the brother of Caleb, took it: and he gave him Achsah his daughter to wife. As she came unto him, she moved him to ask of her father a field: she lighted off her ass; and Caleb said unto her, What wouldest thou? She answered, Give me a blessing; for thou hast given me a south land; give me also springs of water. And he gave her the upper springs, and the nether springs. Guidance: Caleb does what he asked: he drives out the three named Anakim and takes Debir as well. He sets up his own succession by offering his daughter to whoever takes Kirjath-sepher — and a younger relative, Othniel (Israel's first judge in Judges 3), wins the contest. Then Achsah moves the story: she will not settle for dry land. 'Give me a blessing… give me springs.' Caleb the giant-slayer is bested by his own daughter's bold ask — and gladly. The upper and lower springs both. Bold daughters of God may ask for the whole thing.

This is the inheritance of the tribe of Judah according to their families. The uttermost cities of Judah toward the coast of Edom southward were Kabzeel, Eder, Jagur, Kinah, Dimonah, Adadah, Kedesh, Hazor, Ithnan, Ziph, Telem, Bealoth, Hazor-Hadattah, Kerioth, Hezron (which is Hazor), Amam, Shema, Moladah, Hazar-gaddah, Heshmon, Beth-palet, Hazar-shual, Beer-sheba, Bizjothjah, Baalah, Iim, Azem, Eltolad, Chesil, Hormah, Ziklag, Madmannah, Sansannah, Lebaoth, Shilhim, Ain, Rimmon: all the cities twenty and nine, with their villages. Guidance: A roll-call of the south country — twenty-nine specific towns named, including Beer-sheba (where Abraham swore by the LORD) and Ziklag (where David, generations later, will lodge as a fugitive). Even the dry inheritance list has narrative anchors hidden in it. The southland is itemized because God's gifts are not in round numbers; they are in specific addresses.

In the valley, Eshtaol, Zoreah, Ashnah, Zanoah, En-gannim, Tappuah, Enam, Jarmuth, Adullam, Socoh, Azekah, Sharaim, Adithaim, Gederah, Gederothaim — fourteen cities with their villages. Zenan, Hadashah, Migdal-gad, Dilean, Mizpeh, Joktheel, Lachish, Bozkath, Eglon, Cabbon, Lahmam, Kithlish, Gederoth, Beth-dagon, Naamah, Makkedah — sixteen cities with their villages. Libnah, Ether, Ashan, Jiphtah, Ashnah, Nezib, Keilah, Achzib, Mareshah — nine cities with their villages. Ekron, with her towns and her villages: from Ekron unto the sea, all that lay near Ashdod, with their villages. Ashdod with her towns and her villages, Gaza with her towns and her villages, unto the river of Egypt, and the great sea, and the border thereof. Guidance: The Shephelah — the rolling lowland between hill country and Philistine plain. Zoreah and Eshtaol will be Samson's homeland. Adullam is where David will hide from Saul in a cave. Lachish will hold out against the Assyrians for weeks. Each city is a future stage. Inheritance writes the playbook of generations that have not yet been born. The narrator also notes Ekron, Ashdod, Gaza — Philistine cities on Judah's deeded border. Possessed on paper, but not yet on the ground; the chapter has been honest about which lines are taken and which remain to take.

In the mountains: Shamir, Jattir, Socoh, Dannah, Kirjath-sannah (which is Debir), Anab, Eshtemoh, Anim, Goshen, Holon, Giloh — eleven cities with their villages. Maon, Carmel, Ziph, Juttah, Jezreel, Jokdeam, Zanoah, Cain, Gibeah, Timnah — ten cities with their villages. Halhul, Beth-zur, Gedor, Maarath, Beth-anoth, Eltekon — six cities with their villages. Kirjath-baal (which is Kirjath-jearim), and Rabbah — two cities with their villages. Guidance: The hill country — Judah's spine. Hebron is named again under its old title, Kirjath-arba. In the wilderness: Beth-arabah, Middin, Secacah, Nibshan, the City of Salt, and En-gedi — six cities with their villages. Guidance: Only six villages on Judah's eastern slope, between the hill country and the Dead Sea. En-gedi — 'spring of the kid' — will be where David spares Saul's life in a cave (1 Sam 24). Even the most sparsely-settled stretch of inheritance is named: God's portion is not just the fertile cities but also the wilderness rim. Faith claims even what looks like the least.

As for the Jebusites, the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the children of Judah could not drive them out: but the Jebusites dwell with the children of Judah at Jerusalem unto this day. Guidance: The chapter's stinging last verse. Judah held a deed to Jerusalem from verse 8 — but did not finally take it. 'Could not' — partly inability, perhaps partly unwillingness. David will finally seize the citadel four centuries later (2 Sam 5). The line 'unto this day' tells the cost of incomplete obedience: it persists. Inheritance on paper is not inheritance in fact. Every generation has a Jebusite — the assignment partly held, partly tolerated. Future faithfulness is needed to finish what present compromise leaves undone.
